question: We're on mission 7 in the logbook and next to the red 3 there's a purple tile with an omega (Ω) symbol on it. What does that mean — does that task have to be won on the very last trick?
gold_answer: The Ω is a task token. Mission 7 uses 3 task cards, and the Ω token is assigned to one of them: whoever takes that task must fulfill it last, after all the other tasks have been completed. It does NOT have to be won on the last trick of the hand — the rulebook makes the same distinction for the numbered tokens (token 1 doesn't mean 'in the first trick', just 'first of the pending tasks'), and the mission ends successfully as soon as all tasks are completed, even with cards still in hand. Fulfilling it before another pending task, though, immediately fails the mission.

gold_citations.region: mission 7 entry, top-left of spread; red starburst '3' with purple Ω tile below it
mission 12 entry — Ω token also occurs here (same physical spread as mission 7)
mission 28 entry — third Ω occurrence
'Explanation of Symbols in the Logbook' — task-token table (Ω: 'The task must be fulfilled last')
mission ends as soon as all tasks are completed

notes: Icon cross-reference: Ω appears unlabeled in the logbook mission entry (also missions 12 and 28); its meaning is only defined in the rulebook's symbol table on p14. Verified from rendered pages: logbook PDF p4 = printed pages 6-7 (spreads). An earlier draft wrongly placed Ω in mission 9 — mission 9 is '1 Trick with' 1-value cards. Gold citations accept any logbook Ω occurrence (missions 7, 12, 28) plus the Rules definition pages: the icon dictionary injects the meaning at every occurrence, and each is a valid visual cross-check for the user.

question: In mission 21 there's that green communication token printed next to the tasks with a white question mark beside it. Do we just communicate normally, or what's the question mark about?
gold_answer: That's the 'dead zone' symbol — communication is limited this mission. You may still communicate one card, and it still has to be the highest, only, or lowest card of that color in your hand, but you are NOT allowed to place your radio communication token on the card to show which of the three applies. Instead you flip the token to its red side and put it next to the card, and the rest of the crew has to use intuition to work out whether it's your highest, only, or lowest card of that suit.

gold_citations.region: mission 21 entry, bottom-left of spread; green radio token with white '?' oval
'Dead zone' section at top of page, incl. sidebar note

notes: Icon cross-reference: mission 21's flavor text mentions a 'reception dead spot' but never explains the rule — the '?' icon must be matched to the Dead zone section on Rules p17. Distinct from the disruption symbol (red lightning bolt + number, same page) and from the crossed-out radio token (mission 11 art, mission-specific). Verified from rendered pages; logbook PDF p6 = printed 10-11.

question: We just got to mission 25 and the pentagon with the mission number is suddenly outlined in gold, which earlier missions didn't have. Does that change anything for us? We're playing with four.
gold_answer: With four players it changes nothing — the golden frame is only a reminder of the additional rule for five players, which applies from mission 25 onward. With five players, on golden-framed missions, after the playing cards and task cards are distributed but before the first trick starts, one crew member may hand over one of their own task cards to another crew member. You decide together who hands over a card and who takes it, without revealing anything about your cards, and using the rule does not increase the number of attempts recorded in the logbook. (It may also be used in missions 27 and 37.)

gold_citations.region: mission 25 entry, top-left of spread; gold-framed black pentagon '25'
'How to use this Logbook' legend — golden frame callout on the mission-number pentagon
'Playing with Five Players' section + sidebar with gold pentagon '25' icon
continuation: hand-over procedure, missions 27 and 37, no extra attempt

notes: Icon cross-reference spanning both documents: the gold frame is an unlabeled visual difference in the mission entry; its meaning lives in the logbook legend (printed p3) and the rulebook's five-player section (p18-19). Golden frames visually confirmed on missions 25, 27, 28, 30. Verified from rendered pages.

question: The adult red dragon is about to use its Fire Breath on us — can my wizard use counterspell to stop it?
gold_answer: No. Counterspell only works against a creature casting a spell — its casting time is '1 reaction, which you take when you see a creature within 60 feet of you casting a spell,' and its effect interrupts 'a creature in the process of casting a spell.' A dragon's Fire Breath isn't a spell: in the Monster Manual it's an action ('Fire Breath (Recharge 5–6)') with no spell level, casting time, components, or spell slot. Since nothing is being cast, there's nothing to counter. You could still mitigate the damage other ways (e.g. absorb elements, Evasion), but counterspell can't stop a breath weapon.

gold_citations.region: right column, 'Counterspell' spell entry (3rd-level abjuration)
'Adult Red Dragon' stat block, Actions section, 'Fire Breath' entry

notes: Multi-document: requires the counterspell text (PHB) plus recognizing Fire Breath is an action, not a spell (MM stat block). Books verified as 2014 first printings; ruling is edition-stable. PHB/MM printed page = PDF page - 1.

question: My ranger wants to shoot a goblin that's in melee with our fighter, and the fighter is directly between them. Does the fighter give the goblin cover, and can my arrow accidentally hit the fighter if I miss?
gold_answer: Yes on cover: a creature — 'whether that creature is an enemy or a friend' — provides half cover, so the goblin gets +2 to AC (and Dexterity saves) against your shot. Under the core rules a miss is just a miss and your fighter is never hit. The DMG adds an official optional rule, 'Hitting Cover': if your attack misses the goblin but would have hit it without the +2 from cover, and the attack roll also exceeds the fighter's AC, the fighter is struck instead. So by default the fighter is safe; only if your table uses that optional rule can a narrow miss hit them. (There's no general 'firing into melee' penalty in 5e — disadvantage applies only if an enemy is within 5 feet of you, the archer.)

gold_citations.region: 'Cover' section in Chapter 9: Combat — half cover paragraph
'Hitting Cover' optional rule, Chapter 9: Dungeon Master's Workshop

notes: Multi-document: core cover rule in the PHB, the miss-hits-your-ally mechanic only in the DMG optional rule. 'Hitting Cover' is specific to the 2014 DMG (matches these PDFs). DMG printed page = PDF page (offset 0); PHB printed = PDF - 1.

question: My sorcerer casts misty step as a bonus action. Can I still cast fireball with my action on the same turn?
gold_answer: No. The casting-time rules say that when you cast a spell as a bonus action, 'You can't cast another spell during the same turn, except for a cantrip with a casting time of 1 action.' Fireball is a 3rd-level spell, not a cantrip, so it's off the table that turn. You could pair misty step with an action-cast cantrip like fire bolt, or with a non-spell action (Attack, Dash, etc.).

gold_citations.region: Chapter 10 'Casting a Spell' → 'Casting Time' → 'Bonus Action' subsection

notes: Single-reference moderate question; extremely common table question. Wording is 2014-specific (matches these PDFs); the concrete misty step + fireball answer is the same in the 2024 revision.

question: My Scoundrel has a Move 4 with the little winged-boot Jump icon. There's a damage trap two hexes ahead. Can I jump over it safely? And what if the only hex I can end my movement in is the trap hex itself — do I still avoid it since it's a jump?
gold_answer: You can jump over the trap safely — traps are only sprung when a figure enters their hex with normal or forced movement, and Jump (and Flying) movements are unaffected by traps. But you can't end on it safely: the last hex of a jump is still considered normal movement and must obey the normal movement rules, so landing on the trap hex springs it and you take its effect (a damage trap inflicts 2+L damage, where L is the scenario level). Only a Flying figure could end its movement there without springing the trap.

gold_citations.region: Overlay Tiles → 'Traps' bullet, middle of page, with inline Jump and Flying icons
MOVE section, top of page — Jump paragraph and Flying paragraph

notes: Multi-page + icon: trap-trigger rule on p14, last-hex-of-jump rule on p19; the Jump icon (boot with arced arrow) and Flying icon (wings) appear inline in both places. Printed page = PDF page (offset 0). Verified from rendered pages.

question: My Spellweaver's top action shows the plain fire symbol (so it creates fire), and my bottom action on the same turn shows the fire symbol covered by a red X with ': WOUND' after it. Can I create the fire with my first action and immediately consume it with my second action this turn? If not, when can it be used and how long does it last?
gold_answer: No — the rulebook says explicitly that it is not possible to create an element and consume it on the same turn. A plain element symbol means the token only moves to the 'Strong' column at the end of the turn in which you used the ability. Anyone acting later in initiative order that same round (allies or even monsters) can consume it, moving it to 'Inert'. If nobody uses it, it wanes at the end of every round — Strong to Waning, then Waning to Inert — so untouched it lasts through the following round before disappearing.

gold_citations.region: ELEMENTAL INFUSIONS section, bottom of page — infusion icon definition and end-of-turn timing
waning at end of round (top), consumption rules with red-X icon (middle), element symbol key (bottom)

notes: Multi-page + icon: creation timing on p23, same-turn prohibition/consumption/waning on p24. Icons: plain colored element circle = create; same symbol under a red X = consume. Verified from rendered pages.

question: I'm attacking a monster that has Shield 3 with an ability that says Attack 3, PIERCE 2. Before drawing a modifier card, how much damage am I actually doing — and is pierce applied before or after the shield?
gold_answer: Two damage before the modifier card. Pierce ignores up to that many points of the target's Shield for the attack: PIERCE 2 against Shield 3 leaves 1 effective Shield, so Attack 3 deals 3 − 1 = 2 damage, then your attack modifier card adjusts that. Unlike other attack effects, PIERCE is applied while calculating the attack damage rather than afterwards — the rulebook works through this exact example.

gold_citations.region: ATTACK EFFECTS section, 'PIERCE X' entry with the worked Attack 3 / PIERCE 2 / Shield 3 example
SHIELD section, bottom of page (supporting definition)

notes: Moderate single-reference: the rulebook's own example mirrors the question. Verified from rendered pages.

question: It's the second Emperor scoring and after retreating 5 spaces I'm at 0 on the Emperor track. The Emperor tile penalty shows 'lose 3 krones / lose 5 VP'. I only have 2 krones — can I pay the 2 I have, or can I choose to lose the 5 VP instead and keep my money?
gold_answer: Neither — you never get a choice, and you can't partially pay. When your marker ends on 0 you must suffer the penalty on the Emperor tile, and where the tile shows two options separated by a slash you must suffer the left option if you can suffer its full effect. With only 2 krones you can't lose the full 3, so instead you suffer the right option and lose 5 VP (this can even drop you below 0 on the score track). You keep your 2 krones. The tile itself spells it out: 'Lose 3 krones. If you have less than 3 krones, lose 5 VP instead.'

gold_citations.region: Emperor Scoring, step 3 'Check', third bullet (bottom half, left column)
Emperor Tiles index, top-left 'A' (krones) tile reward/penalty text

notes: Multi-page: general slash-penalty rule on p11, the specific tile's text in the p20 appendix. 2022 Lookout revised rulebook; printed page = PDF page (offset 0). Verified from rendered pages.

question: My guest's reward box has that icon of a room tile flipping over onto its dark side. If I use it to flip a vacant room and that happens to be the last unoccupied room in one of my colored groups, do I still get the occupancy bonus even though no guest actually moved in? And can I flip an empty space that has no room tile yet?
gold_answer: Yes to the bonus, no to the empty space. The icon means you may occupy an additional room of a color of your choice by turning it to the occupied side — it only works on a room tile that's already prepared (vacant side up); you're flipping an existing tile, not creating one. And the occupancy bonus doesn't care how the last room got occupied: the rulebook says it does not matter what caused the room to be occupied, explicitly citing guest rewards as an example. So flipping the last room of a group triggers that group's bonus (blue = VP, red = krones, yellow = Emperor track), taken immediately after the effect that caused it; each group pays out only once.

gold_citations.region: guest card icon legend, right column — flip-room icon (two tiles with curved arrow)
'Occupancy Bonus' box, bottom half of page
guest card index #82 Senior Councillor (card carrying this icon)

notes: Icon cross-reference: the flip-room icon is defined in the p16 legend, but the occupancy-bonus interaction lives on p9. Verified from rendered pages.

question: I take a die from the 6 action space to copy the prepare-rooms action. The 6 space had three dice on it but the 3 space only has one die left. How many rooms can I prepare — and does it even matter that the 3 space is nearly empty?
gold_answer: Three rooms (and you could boost to four). Taking a [6] die costs 1 krone; you choose any of the other five main actions and carry it out with a strength equal to the number of dice on the 6 action space — not the copied one. The number of dice on the copied space is completely irrelevant; it could even be empty. You can still boost the copied action by paying an additional 1 krone (so up to four rooms here), and you still pay each room's normal floor cost.

gold_citations.region: 'Imitating a Main Action' section with the worked three-dice example

notes: Moderate single-reference; the rulebook's own example nearly mirrors the question. Verified from rendered pages.

question: We're setting up the red OSL Gardermoen scenario and its card shows a snowflake icon with skid marks. What module is that, and how does it work — specifically, if I put a die on that brakes track and my partner can't put a matching die in the opposite space this round, what happens to my die?
gold_answer: That's the Ice Brakes module: the runway is frozen, and its board covers the normal Brakes section of the Control Panel. It works like the normal Brakes track — deploy left to right, and your Speed must still be lower than the Brake marker when you land — but to advance the Brake marker, two dice of the same value must be placed in the spaces above and below the track in the same round. If you place a die on the track and no matching die lands in the opposite space that round, the die is lost: you take it back at the end of the round and the Brake marker doesn't move. Also, unlike the base game, you MUST get the Brake marker all the way past the 5 before the game ends or you lose.

gold_citations.region: OSL Gardermoen red scenario card — icon row with snowflake/skid-marks icon
ICE BRAKES module section, bottom half of page, with paired-dice board diagram
BRAKES section — the normal brakes rules the module modifies

notes: Icon cross-reference within the flight log (scenario card p8 → module rules p5) plus the rulebook's base Brakes rules. Sky Team module rules live in the flight log itself, not the rulebook. Printed page = PDF page in both documents. Verified from rendered pages.

question: We're flying the yellow GIG Galeão scenario, which has a windsock icon on its card. Playing the Wind module: does the wind ring reset each round? And if our Axis is already tilted 2 marks but our Axis dice this round were equal so the Axis didn't move, do we still turn the wind ring?
gold_answer: The wind ring never resets, and yes — you still turn it. Immediately after the Axis phase each round, you turn the blue Airplane token as many spaces as the Axis is currently off centre, even if the Axis did not move this round. With the Axis sitting 2 marks off centre and equal Axis dice, you still turn the wind ring 2 spaces. This compounds because the Axis arrow itself never resets between rounds, so a persistent tilt keeps feeding the wind. Then during the Engine phase you add the wind speed the token points to onto the sum of your Engine dice — in every round, including the final one.

gold_citations.region: GIG Galeão yellow scenario card (middle-left), windsock icon
WIND module, bottom half of page: 'RULE: AXIS PHASE' and 'RULE: ENGINE PHASE'
AXIS section — arrow stays toward highest die; no reset at end of round

notes: Icon cross-reference (scenario card p7 → Wind module p4) plus the rulebook's Axis no-reset rule that makes the interaction bite. BGG thread asks exactly this ('Do the wind dial reset every round?'). Verified from rendered pages.

question: Quick check mid-game: the rules say we can't have more than 3 Coffee tokens. We've already made 3 this game and spent 2 of them — are we capped at 3 for the whole game, or can we brew more?
gold_answer: You can brew more. The limit of 3 is how many Coffee tokens can sit on the Control Panel at once (there are only 3 designated spaces), not a per-game total. Spent tokens go back to the supply next to the board, and future Concentration dice can create them again from that supply. Having spent 2 of your 3, you have 1 on the board and can make up to 2 more right now. Remember: unspent tokens carry over between rounds, either player may spend them, each token shifts a die you're placing by ±1, and values never wrap around (a 1 minus 1 is not a 6).

gold_citations.region: CONCENTRATION section, bottom half of page — Coffee token rules
setup step 9 — Coffee token reserve next to the board

notes: Moderate core-rules question; BGG thread asks exactly this. Verified from rendered pages.

question: Frodo and Sam just passed one of the bonus spaces on the Quest of the Ring track — the one showing a little fortress with a red X on it. What does that space do, and can I save the effect for later in the game?
gold_answer: When you reach or pass a bonus space you may immediately benefit from its effect. The fortress-with-red-X icon means: remove 1 enemy Fortress from any region and return it to your opponent. You can't bank it — the player aid's footnote says Quest of the Ring track effects (like Landmark effects) are immediate effects you may only use one time, when you gain them. Use it now or lose it.

gold_citations.region: QUEST OF THE RING section — 'reach or pass' rule and annotated track image with bonus spaces
QUEST OF THE RING EFFECTS table, fortress-with-red-X entry, plus asterisked footnote at bottom

notes: Multi-document icon cross-reference: the icon is used on the track (rulebook p6) but defined only in the player aid, and the one-time/immediate timing comes from the player aid footnote. The player aid has no printed page numbers (page_num null). Verified from rendered pages.

question: I got the Hobbit alliance token with the Eagle feather on it. Can I use the Eagle as one of the '3 different Race symbols' to trigger another alliance-token pick, or does it only matter at the end?
gold_answer: It only counts toward the Support of the Races victory condition. Both the rulebook and the player aid define the Eagle narrowly: it is an additional Race symbol that counts as 1 of the 6 required for the Support of the Races victory. Alliance picks are triggered only by Race symbols on Green cards in your play area (2 matching, or once per game 3 different) — the Eagle sits on an Alliance token, not a Green card, so it can never help trigger those conditions.

gold_citations.region: RACE EFFECTS — Hobbits panel, first effect (eagle feather icon)
END OF THE GAME — Support of the Races box, blue italic Note on the Eagle symbol
RACE ALLIANCES — trigger conditions require Race symbols on Green cards

notes: Multi-document: no single sentence says 'the Eagle does not count for alliances' — the answer requires combining the Eagle's scoped definition (player aid + rulebook p8 note) with the Green-cards-only alliance trigger (p6). BGG thread asks exactly this. Verified from rendered pages.

question: I want to take a Landmark tile but I don't have all the Skill symbols it requires. Can I pay Coins instead like with cards, and is there any other cost I'm forgetting?
gold_answer: Yes — the Skill-substitution rule covers tiles as well as cards: if you lack required Skills you may pay 1 Coin to the reserve per missing Skill symbol, with no limit per turn. But don't forget that Landmark tiles have an additional Coin cost equal to the number of your Fortress pawns already on the central board — your first tile costs +0 extra, and it goes up from there. (If you hold the Dwarves' alliance token, you ignore that additional Coin cost.)

gold_citations.region: CARD AND TILE COSTS — Skills substitution paragraph and the boxed Landmark-tile surcharge callout
'B. Take a Landmark tile' action

notes: Moderate; both cost rules sit on rulebook p4. Multiple BGG threads ask exactly this. Verified from rendered pages.

question: I am currently concentrating on Bless. If I cast Misty Step on my turn, does the Bless spell end?
gold_answer: No — Bless keeps running. You only lose concentration in three ways: casting another spell that requires concentration, taking damage and failing the Constitution saving throw, or being incapacitated or killed. Misty Step doesn't require concentration (its duration is Instantaneous), so casting it doesn't touch your concentration on Bless. One side effect to remember: because Misty Step is cast as a bonus action, the only other spell you can cast that turn is a cantrip with a casting time of 1 action — but that limits what else you can cast this turn, it does nothing to spells already in effect.

gold_citations.region: Chapter 10, 'Concentration' — bulleted list of the only things that break concentration; 'Normal activity...doesn't interfere'
Misty Step entry: Casting Time 1 bonus action, Duration: Instantaneous (no concentration)
Bless entry: Duration: Concentration, up to 1 minute
'Bonus Action' casting-time rule (supporting: what else you may cast that turn)

notes: Synthesis question: no single passage says 'Misty Step doesn't break Bless' — the answer combines the concentration break-conditions list with both spells' stat blocks. Question authored by project owner (weaker models historically answered it wrong). All four pages verified visually; PHB printed = pdf - 1.

question: Does the AC bonus from a Shield stack with the Barkskin spell, and can I still use Unarmored Defense while Barkskin is active?
gold_answer: They interact, but they don't 'stack' the way most tables assume, because Barkskin is not a bonus — it's a floor. Barkskin says the target's 'AC can't be less than 16, regardless of what kind of armor it is wearing.' Unarmored Defense keeps working: Barkskin is a spell, not armor (a barbarian's Unarmored Defense is 10 + Dex + Con and explicitly still works while using a shield; a monk's requires wearing no armor AND no shield, so a monk with a shield loses theirs). A shield's +2 applies to your normal AC calculation, not on top of the 16. So compute your AC as usual — e.g. barbarian 10 + Dex + Con, +2 for the shield — and while concentrating on Barkskin your effective AC is the HIGHER of that number or 16. Example: unarmored AC 15 + shield = 17, so Barkskin adds nothing; unarmored AC 12 + shield = 14, so you use 16. Also remember Barkskin takes your concentration for its whole duration.

gold_citations.region: Barkskin entry: 'AC can't be less than 16, regardless of what kind of armor it is wearing'; Duration: Concentration
Barbarian 'Unarmored Defense': 10 + Dex + Con; 'You can use a shield and still gain this benefit'
Monk 'Unarmored Defense': wearing no armor and not wielding a shield, 10 + Dex + Wis
Armor chapter, 'Shields': wielding a shield increases your AC by 2; only one shield at a time

notes: Synthesis question: the interaction is stated nowhere — it must be derived from four passages (floor wording + two UD formulas + shield rule). The 'AC = max(calculated, 16)' floor reading is the RAW-consistent ruling (official Sage Advice concurs; Sage Advice is NOT in the index). Commonly misplayed as '16 + 2'. Question authored by project owner. All pages verified visually; PHB printed = pdf - 1.

question: Can you clarify the criteria for mission 40? We're not sure we're reading all the symbols right.
gold_answer: Mission 40 (logbook p. 17) uses 8 task cards — the number in the red starburst — with three task tokens: 1, 2 and 3. The tasks that receive tokens 1, 2 and 3 must be fulfilled first, second and third out of all the pending tasks (not necessarily in the first three tricks); breaking that order immediately fails the mission. The special icon on this mission — a solid X tile with an arrow pointing to a faded X tile — matches the bolded mission text: before you start choosing task cards, you may move one task token onto another task card that has no token. (The logbook's English text calls these 'Order cards' and 'Order tiles' — they're the same things the rulebook calls task cards and task tokens.) Finally, the golden frame around the mission-number pentagon means that with five players the additional five-player rule applies: one crew member may hand over a task card to another before the first trick. You win by completing all 8 tasks in a valid order.

gold_citations.region: mission 40 entry: gold-framed pentagon '40', red starburst '8', tokens 1-3 top right, X-arrow-faded-X icon beside the attempt circles, bolded special rule
'Explanation of Symbols in the Logbook' — numbered task tokens = fulfillment order; 'first out of all the pending tasks' clarification; violating order fails the mission
'How to use this Logbook' legend: mission pentagon, golden frame, task-card starburst
'Playing with Five Players' — the rule the golden frame flags

notes: Deliberately does NOT describe any icon — unlike crew-icon-001/002/003, the system must read the mission entry itself: parse the starburst count, the tokens, the mission-specific X-to-faded-X icon, the golden frame, and bridge the logbook's 'Order cards/Order tiles' terminology to the rulebook's 'task cards/task tokens'. Question style authored by project owner. Verified visually: logbook PDF p9 = printed 16-17.
